title: Exercises: Lattice Fundamentals
description: Hands-on exercises to master lattice mathematics and operations

exercises.title: Complete Lattice Class Implementation
exercises.description: Implement a full-featured Lattice class with essential operations.
exercises.task: Create a Lattice class that computes fundamental properties and validates inputs correctly.
exercises.starterCode:

from typing import List, Tuple, Optional

class Lattice:
    """
    A class representing a mathematical lattice.
    
    A lattice L(B) is the set of all integer linear combinations
    of the columns of basis matrix B.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, basis: np.ndarray):
        """
        Initialize lattice with basis matrix.
        
        Args:
            basis: n×m matrix where columns are basis vectors
                   (n = ambient dimension, m = rank)
        
        Raises:
            ValueError: If basis vectors are linearly dependent
        """

        pass
    
    @property
    def dimension(self) -> int:
        """Return the ambient dimension (n in R^n)."""

        pass
    
    @property
    def rank(self) -> int:
        """Return the rank (number of basis vectors)."""
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def determinant(self) -> float:
        """
        Compute the lattice determinant.
        
        For full-rank: det(L) = |det(B)|
        For non-full-rank: det(L) = sqrt(det(B^T B))
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def contains(self, v: np.ndarray, tolerance: float = 1e-9) -> bool:
        """
        Check if vector v is in the lattice.
        
        v is in L(B) iff B^(-1) @ v has all integer entries.
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def gram_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Compute the Gram matrix G = B^T @ B.
        
        The Gram matrix captures the inner products between
        all pairs of basis vectors.
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def hadamard_ratio(self) -> float:
        """
        Compute the Hadamard ratio (orthogonality measure).
        
        Hadamard ratio = det(L) / prod(||b_i||)
        
        Returns value in (0, 1], where 1 means perfectly orthogonal.
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
hints: Use np.linalg.matrix_rank() to check linear independence
For non-square bases, det(L) = sqrt(det(B^T B))
A vector v is in L iff B^(-1)v has integer entries
Hadamard ratio = det / product of norms

exercises.title: Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization
exercises.description: Implement Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization, essential for lattice algorithms.
exercises.task: Implement the Gram-Schmidt process and compute the μ coefficients used in LLL.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
from typing import Tuple

def gram_schmidt(B: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
    """
    Compute Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization of basis B.
    
    Given basis B = [b_1, ..., b_n], compute:
    - B* = [b_1*, ..., b_n*] orthogonal vectors
    - μ matrix where μ[i,j] = <b_i, b_j*> / <b_j*, b_j*> for i > j
    
    The relationship is: b_i = b_i* + Σ_{j<i} μ[i,j] * b_j*
    
    Args:
        B: n×m basis matrix (columns are basis vectors)
    
    Returns:
        B_star: Orthogonalized basis (same shape as B)
        mu: m×m lower triangular μ matrix
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def verify_gram_schmidt(B: np.ndarray, B_star: np.ndarray, mu: np.ndarray) -> bool:
    """
    Verify that Gram-Schmidt was computed correctly.
    
    Checks:
    1. B* vectors are orthogonal
    2. b_i = b_i* + Σ μ[i,j] * b_j*
    3. span(b_1,...,b_k) = span(b_1*,...,b_k*) for all k
    """

    pass

def compute_gram_schmidt_norms(B: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:

    Compute ||b_i*||^2 for each Gram-Schmidt vector.
    
    These norms are important for LLL analysis.
    """

testCases.input: B = np.array([[1, 1], [0, 1]]); B_star, mu = gram_schmidt(B); verify_gram_schmidt(B, B_star, mu)
B = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]]); B_star, _ = gram_schmidt(B); np.allclose(B_star, B)

hints: The GS vectors span the same subspaces as original vectors

exercises.title: LLL Basis Reduction
exercises.description: Implement the LLL algorithm for lattice basis reduction.
exercises.task: Implement the full LLL algorithm with size reduction and swap steps.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
from typing import Tuple

def lll_reduce(B: np.ndarray, delta: float = 0.75) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    LLL (Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász) lattice basis reduction.
    
    Produces a δ-LLL reduced basis satisfying:
    1. Size-reduced: |μ[i,j]| ≤ 0.5 for all i > j
    2. Lovász condition: ||b_k*||² ≥ (δ - μ[k,k-1]²)||b_{k-1}*||²
    
    Args:
        B: Basis matrix (columns are basis vectors)
        delta: Lovász parameter, typically 0.75 (must be in (0.25, 1))
    
    Returns:
        LLL-reduced basis matrix
    """

    pass

    Check if a basis is LLL-reduced.
    
    Returns:
        (True, 'OK') if reduced
        (False, reason) if not reduced

def lll_quality_improvement(B_original: np.ndarray, B_reduced: np.ndarray) -> dict:

testCases.input: B = np.array([[1, -1000], [0, 1]], dtype=float); B_red = lll_reduce(B); is_lll_reduced(B_red)[0]
B = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]], dtype=float); B_red = lll_reduce(B); np.allclose(B_red, B)

hints: Size reduce: if |μ[k,j]| > 0.5, subtract round(μ[k,j]) * b_j from b_k
If Lovász fails, swap b_k and b_{k-1}, then go back

exercises.title: Babai's Nearest Plane Algorithm
exercises.description: Implement Babai's algorithm for approximate closest vector.
exercises.task: Implement nearest plane decoding, used as a subroutine in lattice-based encryption.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def babai_nearest_plane(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Babai's nearest plane algorithm for approximate CVP.
    
    Given basis B and target vector t, find a lattice point
    close to t using the nearest plane method.
    
    Algorithm:
    1. Compute Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
    2. Starting from the last coordinate:
       - Project t onto the line through b_i*
       - Round to nearest integer coefficient
       - Subtract from t
    
    Args:
        B: Lattice basis (columns are basis vectors)
        target: Target vector to decode
    
    Returns:
        Approximate closest lattice point
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def babai_rounding(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Babai's rounding method (simpler but less accurate).
    
    Simply round the coefficients of t in the basis B.
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def compare_babai_methods(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> dict:
    """
    Compare nearest plane and rounding methods.
    
    Returns distances achieved by each method.
    """
    # Your code here
    pass
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def gram_schmidt_decomp(B: np.ndarray):
    """Return B*, mu, and ||b_i*||^2."""
    n, m = B.shape
    B_star = np.zeros_like(B, dtype=float)
    mu = np.zeros((m, m))
    
    for i in range(m):
        B_star[:, i] = B[:, i]
        for j in range(i):
            mu[i, j] = np.dot(B[:, i], B_star[:, j]) / np.dot(B_star[:, j], B_star[:, j])
            B_star[:, i] -= mu[i, j] * B_star[:, j]
    
    return B_star, mu

def babai_nearest_plane(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    B = np.array(B, dtype=float)
    target = np.array(target, dtype=float)
    n, m = B.shape
    
    B_star, mu = gram_schmidt_decomp(B)
    
    # Compute coefficients in GS basis
    b = target.copy()
    coeffs = np.zeros(m)
    
    for i in range(m - 1, -1, -1):
        norm_sq = np.dot(B_star[:, i], B_star[:, i])
        if norm_sq > 1e-10:
            coeffs[i] = round(np.dot(b, B_star[:, i]) / norm_sq)
        b -= coeffs[i] * B[:, i]
    
    return B @ coeffs

def babai_rounding(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    B = np.array(B, dtype=float)
    target = np.array(target, dtype=float)
    
    # Solve B @ c = target for c, then round
    coeffs = np.linalg.lstsq(B, target, rcond=None)[0]
    rounded_coeffs = np.round(coeffs)
    
    return B @ rounded_coeffs

def compare_babai_methods(B: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray) -> dict:
    target = np.array(target, dtype=float)
    
    np_result = babai_nearest_plane(B, target)
    round_result = babai_rounding(B, target)
    
    np_dist = np.linalg.norm(target - np_result)
    round_dist = np.linalg.norm(target - round_result)
    
    return {
        'nearest_plane_point': np_result,
        'nearest_plane_distance': np_dist,
        'rounding_point': round_result,
        'rounding_distance': round_dist,
        'better_method': 'nearest_plane' if np_dist <= round_dist else 'rounding'
    }

testCases.input: B = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]]); t = np.array([2.3, 3.7]); np.allclose(babai_nearest_plane(B, t), [2, 4])
B = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]]); t = np.array([0.5, 0.5]); v = babai_nearest_plane(B, t); np.linalg.norm(t - v)

hints: Project onto each plane and round the coefficient
Nearest plane is better for reduced bases
Rounding is simpler but can give worse results for skewed bases
